input_path: data\papers\Zech et al. - Model-driven Digital Twins for AECO.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: Physical twin of an entire building in the Architecture, Engineering, Construction and Operations (AECO) domain, comprising conventional building infrastructure (HVAC, lighting, structural elements) enriched with a comprehensive BIM model that includes Building Control System (BCS) components such as sensors, actuators and controllers. The system is suitable for digital twinning because it allows the integration of static design information with live runtime data to support end‑to‑end lifecycle management—from initial design through construction, operation and refurbishment—providing real‑time insights, predictive analytics and decision‑support capabilities.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin provides a suite of services: (1) real‑time monitoring of building performance metrics (temperature, energy consumption, occupancy); (2) prediction and optimization services that analyze historic and streaming data to forecast consumption and recommend energy savings; (3) control services that expose control logic to Facility Managers, enabling remote actuation of HVAC, lighting and other BCS devices; (4) immersive visualization through interactive dashboards that surface status, anomalies and optimization results; (5) custom analytic services that DT Engineers can plug in via extension points, such as custom predictive models or operational analytics tailored to specific occupant groups. The target users are Building Control Engineers, Facility Managers and DT Engineers, with the twin serving as both an operational control interface and a data analytics platform.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: Implementation relies on a model‑driven deployment framework that extends BIM by adding BCS device models and control logic directly within the BIM environment. MDE (Model‑Driven Engineering) principles are used to automatically transform enriched BIM models into structured semantic representations, from which deployable Digital Twin artifacts are generated with minimal manual intervention. Real‑time data exchange mechanisms synchronize the physical building and its digital counterpart, while an interactive visualization layer provides immersive interfaces. The architecture is modular and exposes standardized extension points for custom services and plugins, facilitating integration with third‑party analytics or simulation engines. The framework is supported by the TwinLight and BOREALIS research projects, funded by the Austrian Research Promotion Agency (FFG).
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: Bidirectional, real‑time data exchange between the physical building and its Digital Twin, providing continuous synchronization with minimal latency; the framework supports varying service time scales but does not specify fixed update frequencies or granularity.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The architecture is centralized, centered around a single Digital Twin Framework hub that orchestrates all components; explicit support for multiple twin instances or hierarchical coordination is not described in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The Digital Twin system follows a modular, service‑oriented architecture featuring standardized extension points and runtime configuration management. The framework integrates device modeling, operational monitoring, and analytics into a central hub that facilitates bidirectional communication, orchestrates services, and allows for optional extension by DT engineers.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: Integration with external systems occurs primarily through BIM tools used by building control engineers for device modeling, and via the DT framework’s interfaces for ingesting enriched BIM models and for real‑time data exchange with the physical building. The framework’s standardized extension points and runtime configuration enable interoperability with heterogeneous systems, but specific protocols or standards are not detailed in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The deployment framework transforms enriched BIM models and device configurations through a validation, conversion and configuration pipeline into structured digital twin artefacts. The geometric representation is derived from BIM, while behavioral control logic is defined in the BIM environment by Building Control Engineers. Device metadata in BIM is mapped to twin components. The transformed data is fed into the middleware stack consisting of Eclipse Hono for connectivity and Eclipse Ditto for digital‑twin state persistence. Visualization is handled by Unreal Engine, which renders the 3D scene and exposes the twin through TwinComponent and FeatureParser extension points.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Actuation in the presented prototype focuses on lighting and HVAC, as illustrated by the LightTwinComponent and the illustrative HVACFeatureParser/HVACTwinComponent.  LightTwinComponent drives photometric states (on/off, brightness, color temperature) of UE5 light sources mapped to real LEDs; HVAC components would control heating, ventilation and air‑conditioning units.  Control is exerted remotely through a DittoDevice endpoint exposed via DittoController, with commands sent through Hono.  While explicit numeric ranges (e.g., illuminance in lux or fan speed in RPM) are not disclosed, the system enforces safety constraints by pre‑execution validation (checking that requested values lie within device specifications) and aborts any command that would violate unit bounds or logical limits.  Actuators are addressed by unique identifiers stored in the BIM metadata and propagated through the middleware, yielding fine‑grained remote control at the component level.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Sensors are populated into the BIM model with associated metadata. The framework utilizes Eclipse Hono to transport real‑time sensor payloads to the twin layer. Ditto stores the incoming measurements, making them available for visualization and analytics. The document does not provide explicit sampling frequencies, accuracy specifications, or transmission rates for the sensors.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Model fidelity is achieved through a traceable MDE pipeline that transforms static BIM into operational twin artifacts, ensuring geometric correctness and semantic alignment.  Validation and verification occur at multiple layers: (1) at the BIM enrichment stage to confirm that device metadata is complete; (2) a policy‑based validation in Ditto to enforce value ranges, units, and logical consistency; (3) pre‑execution checks in the DittoController to avoid unsafe actuator commands.  Uncertainty is implicitly acknowledged through the policy rules that define permissible value ranges, but explicit quantification or uncertainty propagation is not reported.  Error detection relies on Hono’s at‑least‑once message delivery and Ditto’s persistent event storage, which together allow replay and audit of missed updates.  Quality assurance is further supported by modular extension points that enable custom analytics services to be added to the twin, allowing for continuous validation against domain‑specific benchmarks.  The overall process supports a high‑level confidence that the twin remains synchronized, safe, and faithfully represents the physical counterpart, though detailed statistical QA procedures are not described in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Data from physical building sensors and control systems is streamed in real‑time to the digital twin using Eclipse Hono for device messaging and Eclipse Ditto for entity representation. The integration layer continually synchronizes sensor states and operational telemetry, ensuring a live consistency between the physical asset and its digital counterpart.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Control commands issued by facility managers through the digital twin interface are transmitted back to the physical building systems. The bidirectional communication framework provided by Eclipse Hono and Ditto allows these command messages to reach the building control system and confirm execution, enabling real‑time control of physical equipment via the DT.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The DT’s technical backbone is composed of Eclipse Hono as the messaging middleware and Eclipse Ditto as the digital twin broker. Hono orchestrates the transport of sensor telemetry using industry‑standard protocols (e.g., MQTT, AMQP, CoAP) while Ditto manages the state and event synchronization of the twin entities. The layered architecture ensures low latency, scalability and provides a unified API surface for integration with BIM tooling and BCS services.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The framework defines a four‑phase lifecycle for a digital twin: 1) *Device and component modeling* – Building Control Engineers enrich BIM models with sensors, actuators, and controllers to capture Building‑Control‑System (BCS) components, addressing BIM’s static representation limitation. 2) *Transformation* – Model‑Driven Engineering (MDE) principles automatically extract structured semantic representations from the enriched BIM, generating deployable artifacts with minimal manual intervention. 3) *Deployment* – The generated artefacts are provisioned into the operational environment, creating a live digital twin that is ready for interaction. 4) *Operation and evolution* – Real‑time data exchange synchronizes the physical building and the twin, while interactive visualization tools allow Facility Managers to monitor system states and operate physical infrastructure. Transition between stages is managed through automated mapping pipelines, and lifecycle governance is enforced by clearly defined stakeholder roles (Build Control Engineer, Facility Manager, DT Engineer) and a modular architecture with standardized extension points.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: Engineering and development follow a model‑driven workflow that begins with BIM‑based device modeling by Building Control Engineers, proceeds through MDE transformation to produce deployable artefacts, and culminates in automated deployment. Custom services and plug‑ins developed by DT Engineers are integrated through defined extension points, enabling continuous evolution of the twin without disrupting operation. The framework embeds quality assurance through automated transformation rules and reproducible deployments, ensuring consistency between the BIM model and the live twin. Validation of the twin is manual for the initial deployment (verifying state synchronization), and subsequent change cycles rely on regression testing of artefacts. Version control and evolution management are not explicitly specified in the documents, but reproducibility is supported by tracking model sources and transformation steps.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The system provides an interactive visualization interface that presents real‑time state information of the building, allowing Facility Managers to monitor and control operations directly through the twin. This dashboard serves as an immediate decision‑support tool by giving situational awareness; it enables operators to detect anomalies, adjust control logic, or reconfigure devices on‑the‑fly. Confidence in the recommendations derives implicitly from the low‑latency, real‑time data exchange layer that continuously synchronizes physical and digital states.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The framework is grounded in AECO‑specific standards by using BIM as the core representation of building assets. It addresses the lack of standardized integration frameworks by providing well‑defined, standardized extension points that enable plug‑in services and analytics to be added consistently. While specific compliance frameworks or certifications are not detailed in the documents, the modular architecture and reliance on BIM ensure interoperability with AECO workflows. The approach also supports bidirectional communication with the physical building, thereby meeting the requirement for interoperability, although the exact protocols are not specified.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
I. I NTRODUCTION


Digital Twins (DT) are cyber-physical information constructs that through carefully designed integration of static
models with runtime data creates a virtual representation
of a (cyber-)physical asset (e.g., a building) for simulation, analysis, and optimization [1], [2]. Crucially, in the
architecture, engineering, construction and operations (AECO)
domain, DTs enable data-driven infrastructure management
and optimization by providing real-time insights, predictive
analytics, and enhanced decision-making capabilities [3]–[7].
This transformation promises tangible benefits such as reduced
operational costs, enhanced decision-making, and improved
stakeholder collaboration along the complete building life
cycle, from initial design to refurbishments and adaptations
during operation [2], [8]–[12]. However, the realization of this
potential substantially hinges on the ability to systematically
and efficiently deploy DTs in complex and heterogeneous
infrastructure environments [11]–[13].
Despite their promise, the deployment of infrastructure DTs
remains elusive in practice [2]. This is primarily due to
the fragmented nature of models and data, domain-specific


This research has received funding from the Austrian Research Promotion
Agency (FFG) under Grant Agreement No.: 898708, TwinLight, and Grant
Agreement No.: 910225, BOREALIS.

TABLE I: Actor Roles in our Model-driven DT Deployment
Framework


**Role** **Description**


Building Control Engineer Models and configures devices, defines the control logic, and initiates
DT deployment from BIM models.
Facility Manager Monitors system states, interacts
with the twin, and operates the
physical infrastructure via the DT.
DT Engineer Develops and integrates custom services and plugins to extend DT
functionality.


Commensurate with the limitations of BIM as a seamless

enabler for DTs, we have developed a comprehensive set of
requirements for our model-driven deployment framework.
These requirements emerge from both theoretical considerations and practical insights gained through our research
projects, TwinLight and BOREALIS (cf. Section II).
To address the gap between BIM’s static representations and
the dynamic nature of DTs, our proposal must support the
complete life cycle of DT deployment and operation. Table II
outlines the use cases to be supported by our framework,
spanning from initial device modeling to the development
of custom DT services atop our proposal. These use cases
reflect the core functionality needed by different stakeholders
(cf. Table I) involved in the DT life cycle, including _Building_
_Control Engineers_ who model devices and implement control
logic, _Facility Managers_ who monitor and control building operations, and _DT Engineers_ who extend functionality through
custom services.

The functional requirements ( **F** _X_ ) detailed in Table III form
the conceptual foundation of our solution, beginning with
device modeling capabilities that address BIM’s fundamental
limitation in representing BCS components. This enables
building control engineers to define sensors, actuators, and
controllers directly within the BIM environment, creating a
unified semantic foundation for subsequent transformation
steps. The framework then leverages MDE principles to extract
structured representations from these enriched BIM models
and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime

The description covers monitoring, optimization, control, visualization, and custom services, correctly identifying the target users and capabilities mentioned in the text. Quotes: 'real-time monitoring, simulation, and life cycle-encompassing optimization', 'Facility Manager... operates the physical infrastructure via the DT', 'standardized extension points to enable modular, service-oriented DT platforms'.

The extraction lists the specific framework, MDE principles, and technologies (Eclipse Hono, Ditto, Unreal Engine) used for implementation, as well as the funding context. Quotes: 'model-driven framework for scalable DT deployment', 'integrates Eclipse Hono and Ditto for real-time data exchange', 'funded by the Austrian Research Promotion Agency (FFG)'.

block_2_docs_retrieved:
I. I NTRODUCTION


Digital Twins (DT) are cyber-physical information constructs that through carefully designed integration of static
models with runtime data creates a virtual representation
of a (cyber-)physical asset (e.g., a building) for simulation, analysis, and optimization [1], [2]. Crucially, in the
architecture, engineering, construction and operations (AECO)
domain, DTs enable data-driven infrastructure management
and optimization by providing real-time insights, predictive
analytics, and enhanced decision-making capabilities [3]–[7].
This transformation promises tangible benefits such as reduced
operational costs, enhanced decision-making, and improved
stakeholder collaboration along the complete building life
cycle, from initial design to refurbishments and adaptations
during operation [2], [8]–[12]. However, the realization of this
potential substantially hinges on the ability to systematically
and efficiently deploy DTs in complex and heterogeneous
infrastructure environments [11]–[13].
Despite their promise, the deployment of infrastructure DTs
remains elusive in practice [2]. This is primarily due to
the fragmented nature of models and data, domain-specific


This research has received funding from the Austrian Research Promotion
Agency (FFG) under Grant Agreement No.: 898708, TwinLight, and Grant
Agreement No.: 910225, BOREALIS.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime



configuration management further enhances adaptability by
enabling adjustments without requiring full redeployment.
Complementing these functional requirements, our nonfunctional requirements ( **NF** _X_ ) address critical aspects of
interoperability across heterogeneous systems, scalability from
individual components to entire infrastructure systems, maintainability through low-overhead updates, and accessibility for
users with varying technical expertise.
This requirements-driven approach provides a systematic
foundation for implementing DTs that bridge the gap between
BIM’s static representations and the dynamic operational
needs of the AECO domain, addressing the challenges identified in Section II. At this, we want to emphasize that these
are not all requirements, but rather those pertinent to crafting a
technological spike solution tailored to the specific challenges
and innovations explored in this work (cf. Section V-C). The
discussion section will revisit these requirements to evaluate
their effectiveness and role in advancing the proposed solution.


_B. Solution Proposal_


Figure 1 (p. 6) presents the conceptual model of our
framework, illustrating how the requirements from Table III
are implemented through coordinated roles and system components. Figure 1 demonstrates the flow from enriched BIM
models to operational DTs, emphasizing the key roles and their
contributions to the DT life cycle.
_Building Control Engineers_, using BIM tools, perform
device modeling and initiate the deployment process. _Facility_
_Managers_ then interact with the deployed DT for real-time
monitoring and control. _DT Engineers_ extend the framework’s
capabilities through service integration. The DT Framework
acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange
and enabling a holistic approach to building life cycle management.


IV. M ODEL  - BASED D EPLOYMENT OF D IGITAL T WINS

_A. Related Work_


DTs are increasingly recognized in the AECO domain
as to their potential in enabling real-time synchronization
between physical assets and their digital counterparts. Recent
research highlights the integration of DTs with BIM, artificial intelligence, and sensor networks to enhance life cycle
management, cost modeling, and operational efficiency [7],

[14], [22]–[24]. Current literature emphasizes the role of
modeling and workflow automation in achieving consistent
and scalable DT implementations, particularly for modular
construction and infrastructure monitoring [6], [13], [25], [26].
Despite its promise, DT adoption remains limited by fragmented, case-specific deployments that often lack standardized
data schemas, robust integration frameworks, and bidirectional
communication protocols [7], [22], [25], [27]. These technical
challenges are compounded by organizational barriers, including insufficient stakeholder alignment, high implementation
costs, and the absence of unified engineering workflows [23],

[28], [29]. Addressing these limitations will require more
than technological advancement by demanding a rethinking
of system and tool integration, stakeholder collaboration, and
governance across the asset life cycle [13].
As for open source DT frameworks, Gil et al. [30] identify 14 frameworks, most of which target industrial or IoT



applications and offer infrastructure-level components rather
than complete, deployable systems. While frameworks like
Eclipse Ditto [16] and BaSyx [31] provide robust connectivity and standard compliance, they require significant manual
configuration, integration of analytics, and orchestration of
components. Out-of-the-box functionality is generally limited,
and usability for non-developers is low.
In the AECO sector, the situation is more restricted [2],

[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.

However, the claim about supporting 'varying service time scales' is not explicitly stated in the provided text. Supported by quotes: 'framework integrates Eclipse Hono and Ditto for real-time data exchange' and 'Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization'.

The extraction correctly identifies the centralized nature of the framework and the absence of information regarding multiple twin instances or hierarchies in the provided text. It explicitly states what is not in the document, which aligns with the 'Very good' criteria for 'Not in Document' claims. Supported by quotes: 'The DT Framework acts as a central hub'.

The description is highly specific, covering the modular architecture, extension points, runtime configuration, and the central hub's role in facilitating communication. It accurately reflects the roles defined in the document. Supported by quotes: 'standardized extension points to enable modular, service-oriented DT platforms', 'Runtime configuration management', and 'DT Framework acts as a central hub'.

The extraction correctly identifies the use of 'BIM tools' for device modeling and the ingestion of 'enriched BIM models' (Section III-B). It also correctly notes the requirement for interoperability with 'heterogeneous systems' (Section III-A). However, it incorrectly states that 'specific protocols or standards are not detailed,' whereas the Abstract explicitly mentions the integration of 'Eclipse Hono and Ditto for real-time data exchange'.

enriched BIM models and device configurations through validation, conversion, and configuration components. These feed

and updates the UE5 scene rendering accordingly. These

HVACFeatureParser and HVACTwinComponent in Figure 3 are
only for illustrative purposes and currently not implemented in
our proposal. Table IV provides a concise overview on these
extension points.
Our framework ensures data integrity through Eclipse
Ditto’s policy-based validation, which enforces value ranges
and units for sensor data, and pre-execution checks for actuator commands to prevent conflicts or unsafe operations.
Fault tolerance is provided by Eclipse Hono’s at-least-once
message delivery and Ditto’s persistent event storage, ensuring resilience against network failures or service disruptions.
These mechanisms collectively maintain synchronization and
operational safety between physical assets and their DTs.

than technological advancement by demanding a rethinking
of system and tool integration, stakeholder collaboration, and
governance across the asset life cycle [13].
As for open source DT frameworks, Gil et al. [30] identify 14 frameworks, most of which target industrial or IoT



applications and offer infrastructure-level components rather
than complete, deployable systems. While frameworks like
Eclipse Ditto [16] and BaSyx [31] provide robust connectivity and standard compliance, they require significant manual
configuration, integration of analytics, and orchestration of
components. Out-of-the-box functionality is generally limited,
and usability for non-developers is low.
In the AECO sector, the situation is more restricted [2],

[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.

_•_ **R2:** An automated deployment process that reduces manual intervention and enables reproducible DT setups ( **C2** ).

_•_ **R3:** Real-time data integration using Eclipse Hono and
Ditto, enabling live synchronization between physical
assets and digital twins ( **C3** ).

_•_ **R4:** A modular architecture with defined extension points
to support scalable, service-oriented DT development in
AECO ( **C4** ).

Together, these contributions aim to operationalize scalable
and interoperable DTs in AECO contexts. By closing the gap
between design-time models and runtime operations, we offer
a sustainable path toward domain-wide DT adoption.
Our work is organized using Design Science Research
(DSR) [59] and provides artifacts to facilitate model-driven deployment of operational control DTs in the AECO domain. We
start with requirements elicitation (cf. Section III-A) and conclude with prototyping and experimentation (cf. Section V).



Our artifacts are developed in response to the following design
science problem [59]:


**Improve** _DT_ _deployment_ _in_ _BIM-based_ _infrastructure_
_projects_ (context)
**by designing** _a model-driven automation framework_ (artifact)
**that satisfies** _semantic integration, real-time connectivity, and_
_extensibility_ (requirement)
**in order to** _enable scalable DT engineering in the AECO_
_domain_ . (goal)


III. BIM- BASED D IGITAL T WINS

BIM is increasingly regarded as the principal modelling
paradigm in the AECO sector, providing a comprehensive
framework for generating, managing, and disseminating digital
building information throughout the entire building life cycle
with policy initiatives and regulatory mandates decisively
fostering and accelerating this development [60]. BIM enables
the creation of semantically rich, object-based 3D models that
incorporate geometric, spatial, and functional characteristics
of building components and their relationships. These models provide a collaborative platform where architects, engineers, and construction professionals can coordinate design
decisions, detect clashes, simulate performance, and generate
documentation [61].
While there are also DTs and industry-specific solutions
for the digital mapping of assets in other industries, such
as the manufacturing industry (e.g. capitalizing on Modelbased Systems Engineering; MBSE) [1], [62], BIM is becoming the standard solution in the construction industry [63],

TABLE I: Actor Roles in our Model-driven DT Deployment
Framework


**Role** **Description**


Building Control Engineer Models and configures devices, defines the control logic, and initiates
DT deployment from BIM models.
Facility Manager Monitors system states, interacts
with the twin, and operates the
physical infrastructure via the DT.
DT Engineer Develops and integrates custom services and plugins to extend DT
functionality.


Commensurate with the limitations of BIM as a seamless

enabler for DTs, we have developed a comprehensive set of
requirements for our model-driven deployment framework.
These requirements emerge from both theoretical considerations and practical insights gained through our research
projects, TwinLight and BOREALIS (cf. Section II).
To address the gap between BIM’s static representations and
the dynamic nature of DTs, our proposal must support the
complete life cycle of DT deployment and operation. Table II
outlines the use cases to be supported by our framework,
spanning from initial device modeling to the development
of custom DT services atop our proposal. These use cases
reflect the core functionality needed by different stakeholders
(cf. Table I) involved in the DT life cycle, including _Building_
_Control Engineers_ who model devices and implement control
logic, _Facility Managers_ who monitor and control building operations, and _DT Engineers_ who extend functionality through
custom services.

The functional requirements ( **F** _X_ ) detailed in Table III form
the conceptual foundation of our solution, beginning with
device modeling capabilities that address BIM’s fundamental
limitation in representing BCS components. This enables
building control engineers to define sensors, actuators, and
controllers directly within the BIM environment, creating a
unified semantic foundation for subsequent transformation
steps. The framework then leverages MDE principles to extract
structured representations from these enriched BIM models
and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime

_A. Related Work_


DTs are increasingly recognized in the AECO domain
as to their potential in enabling real-time synchronization
between physical assets and their digital counterparts. Recent
research highlights the integration of DTs with BIM, artificial intelligence, and sensor networks to enhance life cycle
management, cost modeling, and operational efficiency [7],

[14], [22]–[24]. Current literature emphasizes the role of
modeling and workflow automation in achieving consistent
and scalable DT implementations, particularly for modular
construction and infrastructure monitoring [6], [13], [25], [26].
Despite its promise, DT adoption remains limited by fragmented, case-specific deployments that often lack standardized
data schemas, robust integration frameworks, and bidirectional
communication protocols [7], [22], [25], [27]. These technical
challenges are compounded by organizational barriers, including insufficient stakeholder alignment, high implementation
costs, and the absence of unified engineering workflows [23],

[28], [29]. Addressing these limitations will require more
than technological advancement by demanding a rethinking
of system and tool integration, stakeholder collaboration, and
governance across the asset life cycle [13].
As for open source DT frameworks, Gil et al. [30] identify 14 frameworks, most of which target industrial or IoT



applications and offer infrastructure-level components rather
than complete, deployable systems. While frameworks like
Eclipse Ditto [16] and BaSyx [31] provide robust connectivity and standard compliance, they require significant manual
configuration, integration of analytics, and orchestration of
components. Out-of-the-box functionality is generally limited,
and usability for non-developers is low.
In the AECO sector, the situation is more restricted [2],

[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.

[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.

Recently, Gerber et al. [33] presented an ad-hoc DT for
a Living Lab for a LEED Platinum academic building based
on BIM for monitoring, simulation, and performance analysis.
Their study emphasizes automated data acquisition, normalization, and 3D visualization, while highlighting challenges
in data integration and stakeholder coordination, insights that
directly inform model-driven approaches in DT engineering.
Their results reinforce earlier findings by Opoku et al. [34]
who also demonstrated the effective integration of DTs with
BIM and IoT platforms to optimize indoor environmental conditions in a university library setting. Both studies underscore
the significance of real-time data utilization and visualization
in enhancing facility management and highlight common challenges in data integration and stakeholder engagement in DT
implementations. In synopsis, AECO adoption is dominated by
commercial tools or custom-built solutions [2], [13]. Notably,
MDE is not present across the surveyed works, leaving a
critical gap in abstraction and reuse capabilities essential for
complex domains like AECO.
Model-driven engineering (MDE) offers a streamlined approach to handling the increasing complexity of systems by
elevating models to primary engineering artifacts [35], [36].
It enables abstraction, formalization, and automation across
the development life cycle, reducing cognitive overload and
improving consistency in large-scale systems [37]. MDE supports the definition of domain-specific modeling languages
and metamodels that unify engineering workflows, providing
a structured foundation for communication across disciplines
and life cycle phases [38], [39]. Importantly, MDE also
strengthens governance by encoding organizational policies
and architectural decisions into formal models, promoting
compliance, reuse, and quality assurance [40]. Recent advances continue to refine the integration of MDE into DT
systems [41]–[47]. As reported in Lehner et al.’s recent survey,
research primarily focuses on runtime adaptability, semantic
coherence, and tool interoperability [48]. Abbasi et al. [49]
examined the phenomenon of _semantic drift_, where the alignment between digital models and their physical counterparts
diverges over time. Their study stresses the necessity of
synchronization techniques to ensure the integrity of modelbased DTs. Kamburjan et al. [50] proposed a declarative life
cycle specification language, enabling dynamic evolution and
rule-based management of DT instances. Meanwhile, Oakes et
al. [51] advanced ontological frameworks to support service
